---
title: "Components, workflows & modularity"
description: "The building blocks, and why they are modular at the execution level."
order: 20
---

## The building blocks

Everything in OpenPipeline is assembled from a few simple pieces, all produced by
[Viash](viash.qmd), the engine that turns a plain script into a runnable,
containerized unit. Three words recur throughout the docs:

```{=html}
<div class="concept-list">

  <div class="concept-card">
    <div class="ct">Component</div>
    <p>The fundamental unit: one analysis step, such as normalizing counts or detecting doublets. A component pairs a <code>config.vsh.yaml</code>, which declares its inputs, outputs, and container, with a script (Python, R, or Bash) that does the work. Viash builds the two into a self-contained, containerized tool you can run entirely on its own.</p>
  </div>

  <div class="concept-card">
    <div class="ct">Workflow</div>
    <p>A workflow is itself a component, one whose script is a Nextflow <code>main.nf</code> that chains other steps into a pipeline (ingestion, processing, integration, ...). Because it is just another component, components and workflows are interchangeable: either can be declared as a dependency and reused, so pipelines nest and compose freely.</p>
  </div>

  <div class="concept-card">
    <div class="ct">Module</div>
    <p>The runnable Nextflow form of a component or workflow, auto-generated by Viash. You write components and workflows; Viash produces the modules that Nextflow executes.</p>
  </div>

</div>
```

## Modularity where it counts

Most pipelines call themselves modular because their *source code* is tidy,
functions in files, steps in folders. OpenPipeline is modular at the level that
actually matters for running an analysis: the **execution level**.

## Code-level vs. execution-level modularity

**Code-level modularity** organizes source code: a well-named function, a script
per step. But those pieces are still stitched together by shared imports, a shared
environment, and glue code. Swapping one out means editing the others.

**Execution-level modularity** means each step is an *independently built,
versioned, containerized unit* that runs on its own. In OpenPipeline every
component is:

- **Self-contained**: its dependencies live in its own Docker image, so two
  components can need conflicting library versions and still run in the same
  pipeline.
- **Independently runnable**: you can execute, test, or build any component on
  its own with `viash run` / `viash test`, without the rest of the pipeline.
- **A drop-in unit**: because every component reads and writes the same
  [MuData](mudata.qmd) file through a generated CLI, one can be placed before,
  after, or in place of another with no format conversion and no glue code.

## Why it matters

Building a pipeline becomes composition, not integration. Adding a step,
removing one, or swapping an integration method is a change to the *pipeline
definition*, not a refactor of shared code. A component written for the core
[`openpipeline`](ecosystem.qmd) package can even be declared as a dependency from
another package's repository and reused unchanged, because the unit of reuse is
the whole component, not a snippet of source you copy.

This is what the [engine, Viash and Viash Hub](viash.qmd) is built to deliver,
and what lets the [ecosystem](ecosystem.qmd) of packages share one set of
components.
